content:
# Exodus Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~35v–36r (PDF pp. 69–70); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 49–50 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_009_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_009_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_009_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Fifth Plague: Livestock Disease (vv. 1–7)

**1** And the LORD said to Moses, "Go in to Pharaoh and speak to him, 'Thus says the LORD, the God of the Hebrews: Send out My people, that they may serve Me.

**2** For if you refuse to send them out and you still hold them,

**3** behold, the hand of the LORD is upon your livestock that are in the field — upon the horses, upon the donkeys, upon the camels, upon the cattle, and upon the flocks — a very heavy pestilence.

**4** And the LORD will distinguish between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of Egypt, and nothing shall die of all that belongs to the sons of Israel.'"

**5** And the LORD set an appointed time, saying, "Tomorrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land."

**6** And the LORD did this thing on the next day, and all the livestock of Egypt died; but of the livestock of the sons of Israel, not one died.

**7** And Pharaoh sent, and behold, not even one of the livestock of Israel had died. But the heart of Pharaoh was heavy [**kaved**], and he did not send out the people.

## The Sixth Plague: Boils (vv. 8–12)

**8** And the LORD said to Moses and to Aaron, "Take for yourselves handfuls of soot from a kiln, and let Moses throw it toward the heavens before the eyes of Pharaoh.

**9** And it shall become fine dust over all the land of Egypt, and it shall become boils breaking out in blisters upon man and upon beast in all the land of Egypt."

**10** And they took the soot of the kiln and stood before Pharaoh, and Moses threw it toward the heavens, and it became boils — blisters breaking out upon man and upon beast.

**11** And the magicians were not able to stand before Moses because of the boils, for the boils were upon the magicians and upon all Egypt.

**12** **And the LORD strengthened [chazaq, Piel] the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not listen to them, as the LORD had spoken to Moses.**

## The Seventh Plague: Hail (vv. 13–35)

**13** And the LORD said to Moses, "Rise early in the morning and station yourself before Pharaoh, and say to him, 'Thus says the LORD, the God of the Hebrews: Send out My people, that they may serve Me.

**14** For at this time I am sending all My plagues unto your heart, and upon your servants, and upon your people, so that you may know that there is none like Me in all the earth.

**15** For now I could have stretched out My hand and struck you and your people with the pestilence, and you would have been cut off from the earth.

**16** But for this very reason I have raised you up — to show you My power, and so that My name may be declared in all the earth.

**17** You still exalt yourself against My people by not sending them out.

**18** Behold, about this time tomorrow I am causing to rain a very heavy hail, the like of which has not been in Egypt from the day it was founded until now.

**19** And now, send — bring into shelter your livestock and all that you have in the field. Every man and beast that is found in the field and is not gathered into the house — the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die.'"

**20** The one who feared the word of the LORD among the servants of Pharaoh caused his servants and his livestock to flee into the houses.

**21** But the one who did not set his heart to the word of the LORD left his servants and his livestock in the field.

**22** And the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the heavens, and there shall be hail in all the land of Egypt — upon man, and upon beast, and upon every plant of the field in the land of Egypt."

**23** And Moses stretched out his staff toward the heavens, and the LORD gave thunder and hail, and fire went toward the earth, and the LORD rained hail upon the land of Egypt.

**24** And there was hail, and fire flashing in the midst of the hail — very heavy — the like of which had not been in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation.

**25** And the hail struck in all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, from man to beast. And the hail struck every plant of the field and shattered every tree of the field.

**26** Only in the land of Goshen, where the sons of Israel were, there was no hail.

**27** And Pharaoh sent and called for Moses and for Aaron and said to them, "I have sinned this time. The LORD is the righteous one, and I and my people are the wicked ones.

**28** Entreat the LORD, for there has been enough of the thunder of God and the hail. And I will send you out, and you shall not continue to stay."

**29** And Moses said to him, "When I go out of the city, I will spread out my palms to the LORD. The thunder shall cease and the hail shall be no more, so that you may know that the earth belongs to the LORD.

**30** But as for you and your servants, I know that you do not yet fear before the LORD God."

**31** And the flax and the barley were struck, for the barley was in the ear and the flax was in bud.

**32** But the wheat and the spelt were not struck, for they are late crops.

**33** And Moses went out from Pharaoh, out of the city, and spread out his palms to the LORD. And the thunder and the hail ceased, and rain was not poured out upon the earth.

**34** And Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunder had ceased, and he continued to sin — and he made heavy [**kaved**, Hiphil] his heart, he and his servants.

**35** And the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [**chazaq**], and he did not send out the sons of Israel, as the LORD had spoken by the hand of Moses.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~35v (PDF p. 69) carries the beginning of Exodus 9, following directly from the close of chapter 8 in the right-hand column. The three-column layout continues with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. A petuchah (open paragraph) marker is visible between the livestock plague and the boils narrative, and another petuchah introduces the hail plague section beginning at verse 13. This is consistent with the traditional division of the plague accounts into discrete units. The second page (folio ~36r, PDF p. 70) continues through the hail narrative and into the conclusion of the chapter. Masorah parva notes appear in the margins, including notations on uncommon forms such as mitloseset ("flashing," v. 24) and the rare he'emadtikha ("I have raised you up," v. 16). The Codex Vaticanus pages 49–50 preserve the LXX text of this chapter. Page 49 (marked with a chapter indicator at the top) carries the livestock plague and boils section, and page 50 continues with the hail plague through to its conclusion. The Vaticanus text of Exodus 9 contains several notable expansions and differences from the MT, particularly in verses 15–16 and 19–21, as detailed below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"The God of the Hebrews" (v. 1) — Elohey ha'Ivrim:** This fuller divine title appears here for the first time in the plague cycle (also in v. 13). The designation "Hebrews" ('Ivrim) is used when Israelites are identified from an outsider's perspective or in diplomatic contexts. The term may derive from the ancestor Eber (Gen. 10:21) or the root '-b-r ("to cross over"). It positions YHWH as the covenant deity of a specific people — the Hebrews — over against the gods of Egypt. The LXX renders ho theos tōn Hebraiōn ("the God of the Hebrews").

2. **"The hand of the LORD is upon your livestock" (v. 3) — yad-YHWH hovah:** Hebrew hineh yad-YHWH hovah bemiknekha. The noun hovah (participle of h-y-h, "being, becoming") indicates the hand of the LORD is actively present, already operative. The "hand of the LORD" (yad-YHWH) is a synecdoche for divine power in action — here directed specifically at livestock. This contrasts with the "finger of God" (etsba Elohim) confessed by the magicians in 8:19: what was a "finger" now becomes a full "hand." The word dever ("pestilence") derives from a root meaning "to destroy, to speak a word of judgment" (related to davar, "word/matter"). LOGOS renders "pestilence" to preserve the gravity of the term. The LXX renders thanatos megas sphodra ("a very great death"), intensifying the description.

3. **"The LORD will distinguish" (v. 4) — hiphla:** Hebrew vehiphlah YHWH ben miqneh Yisra'el uven miqneh Mitsrayim. The Hiphil of palah again, as in 8:22 — God "makes a distinction." The root palah carries overtones of wondrous separation. This continues the theological theme introduced in the swarm plague: divine discrimination between Israel and Egypt. LOGOS renders "distinguish" to maintain the formal-equivalence standard. The LXX renders paradoxasei kurios ("the Lord will make a marvel"), preserving the "wonder" dimension.

4. **"The LORD set an appointed time" (v. 5) — mo'ed:** Hebrew vayyasem YHWH mo'ed. The noun mo'ed ("appointed time, fixed time, meeting") derives from the root y-'-d ("to appoint, to designate"). God does not merely announce what will happen — He fixes a precise time for it. The mo'ed transforms the plague from a vague threat into an ordered, calendrical event under divine sovereignty. LOGOS renders "appointed time." The LXX renders prothesmen ("an appointed limit/time").

5. **"The heart of Pharaoh was heavy" (v. 7) — kaved, adjective:** Hebrew vayyikhbad lev Par'oh. This is the Qal stative/adjectival use of kaved — "the heart of Pharaoh was heavy" — a description of state rather than an act. Unlike the Hiphil forms in 8:15 and 8:32 where Pharaoh actively "made heavy" his heart, here the text describes a condition: his heart was heavy. No agent is named; the heaviness is presented as a settled condition. LOGOS renders "was heavy" to mark the distinction from the causative Hiphil. The LXX renders ebarunthē hē kardia Pharaō ("the heart of Pharaoh was made heavy").

6. **"Soot of a kiln" (v. 8) — piach kivshan:** Hebrew melo chophneykhem piach kivshan. The piach ("soot, ashes") is the fine residue from a kivshan (lime kiln or furnace). The word kivshan may deliberately evoke the furnaces/brick kilns in which the Israelites labored (cf. 1:14; 5:7–8) — the instrument of their oppression becomes the means of Egypt's affliction. The throwing of soot "toward the heavens" (hashamayemah) before Pharaoh's eyes is a prophetic sign-act: what rises up from the kilns of slavery descends as plague upon the oppressors. LOGOS renders "soot from a kiln." The LXX renders aithalēn kaminiaian ("soot of a furnace"), using kaminos, the word for furnace.

7. **"Boils breaking out in blisters" (v. 9) — shechin pore'ach avavbu'ot:** Hebrew shechin pore'ach avavbu'ot. The shechin is a skin inflammation or ulcerous boil (cf. Lev. 13:18–23; Deut. 28:27, 35; Job 2:7). The participle pore'ach ("breaking out, flowering") from the root p-r-ch ("to bud, to blossom") — the same root used for plants budding — creates a grotesque botanical metaphor: the boils "bloom" like flowers. The avavbu'ot ("blisters, pustules") is a rare word appearing only here and in v. 10. This is a disease that flowers on the skin. LOGOS preserves all three Hebrew terms in translation. The LXX renders helkē phlyktides ("sore blisters/pustules"), using medical terminology.

8. **"The magicians were not able to stand before Moses" (v. 11):** Hebrew velo-yakhlu hachartummim la'amod lipney Moshe mipney hasshechin. This is the magicians' final appearance in the plague narrative. Their trajectory is now complete: imitation (7:11, 22; 8:7), failure (8:18), confession (8:19), and now personal affliction — they cannot even stand (la'amod, "to take their stand") in Moses' presence. The verb 'amad ("to stand") implies maintaining one's position of authority or dignity; they are stripped of both. That the boils afflict the magicians specifically demonstrates that their sorcery provides no protection even for themselves. LOGOS renders literally to emphasize their humiliation. The LXX reads kai ouk ēdunanto hoi pharmakoi stēnai enantion Mōusē ("and the sorcerers were not able to stand before Moses").

9. **"And the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" (v. 12) — THE FIRST DIVINE HARDENING:** Hebrew vaychazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh. **This is the pivotal moment in the hardening narrative.** For the first time, the verb chazaq appears in the Piel (intensive-causative) stem with YHWH as the explicit grammatical subject and Pharaoh's heart as the direct object. The Piel of chazaq means "to make strong, to strengthen" — with intensive force. Up to this point, the hardening verbs have been: (a) God's prospective announcement that He will harden (7:3, qashah Hiphil), (b) Pharaoh's heart strengthening itself in the Qal stative with no named agent (7:13, 22; 8:19), and (c) Pharaoh actively making his own heart heavy in the Hiphil (8:15, 32). Now, for the first time, God acts directly upon Pharaoh's heart. The text says "as the LORD had spoken to Moses" — referring back to 4:21 and 7:3, where God predicted this very outcome. What was announced is now enacted. This is the fulfillment of the divine prediction, and it marks a transition in the narrative: from this point forward, the text will alternate between Pharaoh's self-hardening and God's hardening, with God's active hardening becoming increasingly prominent. LOGOS renders "the LORD strengthened" to maintain the chazaq = "strengthen" convention, using bold formatting to mark this critical transition. The LXX renders kai esklērunen kurios tēn kardian Pharaō ("and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh"), using sklērunō ("to harden"), a stronger term than the earlier katischuō ("to be strong").

10. **"All My plagues unto your heart" (v. 14) — kol-maggephothay el-libbeka:** Hebrew ki bapa'am hazot ani shole'ach et-kol-maggephothay el-libbeka. The phrase "unto your heart" (el-libbeka) is remarkable — the plagues are directed not merely upon Pharaoh's body or land but unto his heart (lev). Given the centrality of Pharaoh's heart in the hardening motif, this targeting is deliberate: God sends plagues to the very organ that Pharaoh has hardened. The noun maggephah ("plague, blow, striking") derives from nagaph ("to strike, to smite"). LOGOS renders "unto your heart" preserving the Hebrew preposition el ("unto, to, toward") rather than the weaker "against." The LXX renders eis tēn kardian sou ("into your heart"), preserving the directional sense.

11. **"I have raised you up" (v. 16) — he'emadtikha:** Hebrew ve'ulam ba'avur zot he'emadtikha ba'avur har'otkha et-kochi ulema'an sapper shemi bekhol-ha'arets. The Hiphil of 'amad ("to stand") means "to cause to stand, to raise up, to establish." This verse declares that God has raised up Pharaoh — caused him to stand in his position — for a specific purpose: to demonstrate divine power and to have God's name declared throughout the earth. This does not mean God created Pharaoh's wickedness, but that God has sustained and established Pharaoh in his role so that the confrontation may serve God's revelatory purposes. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:17 (from the LXX): "For this very purpose I raised you up (exēgeira se), that I might show My power in you." Note that Paul's LXX-based quotation uses exegeirō ("to raise up, to bring onto the scene") rather than the MT's he'emadtikha ("I caused you to stand"). The LXX of Exodus 9:16 reads heneken toutou dietērēthēs ("for this reason you have been preserved/kept alive"), using diatēreō, which implies preservation rather than establishment — a significantly different nuance from both the MT and Paul's quotation. LOGOS renders "I have raised you up" following the MT's he'emadtikha.

12. **"You still exalt yourself" (v. 17) — mistolel:** Hebrew odekha mistolel be'ammi. The Hitpolel (reflexive) participle of salal ("to heap up, to raise up") means "to exalt oneself, to set oneself up." The form is denominative — Pharaoh is heaping himself up, building himself into an obstacle against God's people. The ironic contrast with verse 16 is pointed: God "raised up" (he'emadtikha) Pharaoh, but Pharaoh "exalts himself" (mistolel) against God's people. LOGOS renders "exalt yourself" to capture the reflexive force. The LXX renders emporieuē ("you deal treacherously with, you traffic in") — a different interpretive direction, reading the verb as commercial exploitation.

13. **"Fire flashing in the midst of the hail" (v. 24) — esh mitlaqqachath betokh habbarad:** Hebrew ve'esh mitlaqqachath betokh habbarad. The Hitpael participle mitlaqqachath from laqach ("to take") means "taking itself, flashing" — fire that seizes and releases, flashing repeatedly. This is a supernatural phenomenon: fire and ice coexisting. The Midrash (Exodus Rabbah 12:4) marvels that fire and hail made peace with each other to accomplish God's purpose. LOGOS renders "fire flashing" to capture the iterative Hitpael sense. The LXX renders pur phlegon ("fire burning/blazing"), which simplifies the Hebrew's reflexive iterative nuance.

14. **"The LORD is the righteous one" (v. 27) — YHWH hatsaddiq:** Hebrew YHWH hatsaddiq va'ani ve'ammi haresha'im. This is Pharaoh's first confession, and it is theologically precise — he uses the definite article: "the righteous one" (hatsaddiq), not merely "righteous." The adjective tsaddiq denotes conformity to a standard, being in the right, fulfilling obligations — it is a legal-covenantal term. Pharaoh simultaneously confesses his own status as rasha' ("wicked one"), which denotes being in the wrong, guilty before a tribunal. This is courtroom language: YHWH is vindicated; Pharaoh is condemned. Yet this confession, however accurate, proves insincere — Pharaoh reverses himself as soon as the hail stops (vv. 34–35). LOGOS renders with the definite article to preserve the Hebrew syntax: "the righteous one ... the wicked ones." The LXX renders ho dikaios ... hoi asebeis ("the righteous one ... the ungodly ones"), using asebēs ("impious, ungodly") rather than a strict equivalent to rasha'.

15. **"The thunder of God" (v. 28) — qolot Elohim:** Hebrew qolot Elohim. Literally "voices of God" or "sounds of God." The noun qol (plural qolot) means "voice, sound, thunder." The use of Elohim as a modifier can be a superlative — "mighty thunder" — or a literal attribution: "God's thunder," that is, thunder that comes directly from God. Given the theophanic context (thunder, hail, fire), the literal reading is preferable: this is God's own voice manifest in the storm. LOGOS renders "thunder of God" preserving the genitive. The LXX renders phōnēn theou ("voice/sound of God").

16. **"The flax and the barley were struck" (vv. 31–32):** Hebrew vehapishtah vehas'orah nukkathah ki has'orah aviv vehapishtah giv'ol. This agricultural notice provides a seasonal marker: the barley was aviv ("in the ear," i.e., the grain heads were formed) and the flax was giv'ol ("in bud," i.e., the stem was forming its seed capsules). This places the hail plague in approximately January–February in the Egyptian agricultural calendar, as barley ears and flax buds form in that period. The wheat (chittah) and spelt (kussemet) were not struck because they are aphilot ("late crops," literally "dark, hidden") — still underground or barely sprouting. This incidental agricultural detail provides remarkable verisimilitude. LOGOS preserves each Hebrew botanical term. The LXX renders the passage similarly, though it translates giv'ol as spermatizousa ("seeding").

17. **"He made heavy his heart" (v. 34) — kaved, Hiphil + chazaq double hardening:** Hebrew vayyosef lachato vayyakhbed libbo hu va'avadav. And then v. 35: vayyechezaq lev Par'oh. Verse 34 contains two significant elements: (a) the phrase vayyosef lachato ("he continued to sin") — the narrator explicitly labels Pharaoh's hardening as sin (chet) for the first time; and (b) vayyakhbed libbo ("he made heavy his heart"), the Hiphil of kaved with Pharaoh as active agent, consistent with 8:15 and 8:32. The addition of hu va'avadav ("he and his servants") extends the hardening to Pharaoh's court. Then in v. 35, vayyechezaq lev Par'oh ("the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened") — this is the Qal stative of chazaq, the heart as grammatical subject, no divine agent named. The double hardening in verses 34–35 (kaved Hiphil followed by chazaq Qal) creates a comprehensive closing: Pharaoh actively weighs down his heart, and the result is a heart that is firm, unyielding. LOGOS renders both verbs distinctly to preserve the two-verb sequence.

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 9

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | Subject/Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9:7 | **kaved** | Qal (stative/adj.) | Pharaoh's heart was heavy | "the heart of Pharaoh was heavy" |
| 9:12 | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart** | **"the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** |
| 9:34 | **kaved** | Hiphil (causative) | Pharaoh makes his own heart heavy | "he made heavy his heart" |
| 9:35 | **chazaq** | Qal (stative) | Pharaoh's heart was strengthened | "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" |

*Note: Exodus 9 marks the decisive turning point in the hardening narrative. In v. 7, after the livestock plague, Pharaoh's heart is simply described as "heavy" (kaved Qal) — a state, not an action. Then in v. 12, after the boils, the narrator records what had been long announced but never enacted: YHWH Himself "strengthened" (chazaq Piel) Pharaoh's heart. This is the fulfillment of the divine declarations in 4:21 and 7:3. The Piel stem marks intensive causative agency — God does not merely observe the hardening but actively effects it. Yet the chapter does not end there. In vv. 34–35, after Pharaoh's own confession of YHWH's righteousness (v. 27), Pharaoh returns to self-hardening: he "makes heavy" (kaved Hiphil) his heart and it "is strengthened" (chazaq Qal). The interplay is theologically complex: divine hardening and human self-hardening coexist within the same chapter. The narrator also introduces a new category in v. 34 — "he continued to sin" (vayyosef lachato) — explicitly labeling the hardening as moral transgression.*

### Cumulative Hardening Verb Table — Exodus 7–9

| Verse | Root | Form | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:3 | qashah | Hiphil | God (prospective) | "I will make hard" — announced, not yet enacted |
| 7:13 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after staff sign |
| 7:14 | kaved | Adjective | — (descriptive) | "is heavy" — God's diagnosis |
| 7:22 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after blood plague |
| 8:15 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after frogs removed |
| 8:19 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after gnats; magicians confess |
| 8:32 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after swarm removed |
| 9:7 | kaved | Qal (adj.) | Heart (descriptive) | "was heavy" — after livestock plague |
| **9:12** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — FIRST DIVINE HARDENING** |
| 9:34 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after hail; labeled "sin" |
| 9:35 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — chapter closing |

### Magicians (Chartummim) Tracking — Final Appearance

| Verse | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 9:11 | Afflicted by boils | Cannot stand before Moses — personal humiliation complete |

*Note: Exodus 9:11 is the magicians' final appearance in the plague narrative. Their complete trajectory across chapters 7–9 is: successful imitation (7:11, staff-to-serpent; 7:22, water-to-blood; 8:7, frogs), failed imitation (8:18, gnats), confession of divine power (8:19, "the finger of God"), and now personal affliction (9:11, boils). The text emphasizes their inability to "stand" (la'amod) — a word that resonates with he'emadtikha ("I have raised you up / caused to stand") in v. 16. Those whom Egypt's wisdom "caused to stand" in positions of authority cannot now stand at all. They disappear from the narrative entirely after this point, having served as a foil that progressively magnifies God's power.*

### Key Term Renderings — Exodus 9

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| dever | pestilence | vv. 3, 15; root d-b-r "to destroy/speak judgment" |
| piach kivshan | soot from a kiln | v. 8; kiln soot; evokes Israelite slave labor |
| shechin | boils | vv. 9–11; skin inflammation, ulcerous sore (cf. Lev. 13, Job 2) |
| avavbu'ot | blisters | vv. 9–10; rare: pustules that "blossom" from the boils |
| pore'ach | breaking out / flowering | vv. 9–10; Qal participle of p-r-ch "to bud" |
| he'emadtikha | I have raised you up | v. 16; Hiphil of 'amad; quoted by Paul in Rom. 9:17 |
| mistolel | exalt yourself | v. 17; Hitpolel of salal "to heap up" |
| barad | hail | vv. 18–35; the central plague element |
| mitlaqqachath | flashing | v. 24; Hitpael of laqach "to take" — fire seizing itself |
| tsaddiq / rasha' | righteous one / wicked ones | v. 27; legal-covenantal terms; Pharaoh's confession |
| qolot Elohim | thunder of God | v. 28; lit. "voices of God"; theophanic storm |
| aviv | in the ear | v. 31; barley grain stage; seasonal marker (Jan.–Feb.) |
| giv'ol | in bud | v. 31; flax bud stage |
| aphilot | late crops | v. 32; lit. "dark, hidden"; wheat and spelt still underground |
| kussemet | spelt | v. 32; emmer wheat; a hardier late-season grain |

---

### Theological Note: The Architecture of Exodus 9

Exodus 9 is architecturally significant within the plague narrative. It contains the transition from human self-hardening to divine hardening (v. 12), the theological purpose statement for Pharaoh's existence (v. 16), the first explicit labeling of hardening as "sin" (v. 34), and Pharaoh's first confession of YHWH's righteousness (v. 27). The three plagues in this chapter also demonstrate escalating severity: livestock die (economic devastation), humans are afflicted with boils (bodily suffering), and the unprecedented hail destroys crops and kills those caught in the open (comprehensive destruction). The warning system also evolves: in the livestock plague, God gives a time frame (v. 5, "tomorrow"); in the hail plague, God offers an opportunity to seek shelter (v. 19) — introducing for the first time the possibility of individual Egyptians responding to the divine word in faith ("the one who feared the word of the LORD," v. 20). This creates a category within Egypt itself: those who fear YHWH's word and those who do not — a distinction that transcends nationality and anticipates the "mixed multitude" (erev rav) of 12:38.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 9 (pages 49–50) presents several significant divergences from the MT. Most notably, in v. 16, where the MT reads he'emadtikha ("I have raised you up / caused you to stand"), the LXX reads heneken toutou dietērēthēs ("for this reason you have been preserved alive") — a substantially different claim (preservation rather than establishment). Paul's quotation in Romans 9:17 uses yet a third formulation: exēgeira se ("I raised you up / brought you onto the scene"), which is closer to the MT's sense than the LXX's. This three-way divergence (MT, LXX, Paul) is one of the most discussed text-critical issues in Exodus. In v. 19, the Vaticanus text shows an expansion of the warning to shelter livestock, with additional phrasing about gathering possessions. The Vaticanus manuscript on page 50 shows clear section markings at the transition points between plagues. The LXX's rendering of the hardening verb in v. 12 uses sklērunō ("to harden"), which is more intense than the katischuō ("to be strong") used in earlier chapters — the LXX translators appear to mark the same transition that the MT marks by shifting from Qal to Piel. Overall, the LXX of chapter 9 is theologically significant precisely where it diverges most from the MT, particularly in the purpose statement of v. 16, which generated a rich interpretive tradition in Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity.

--- LOGOS
